text: The effect of pH on the availability of nutrients to plants is not due solely to its effect on their reactions with soil but also with its effect on plant uptake. Some of the effects appear to be specific to particular ions while other effects are more general and associated with the characteristics of the variable charge surfaces of both plant roots and soil particles. An example of a nutrient for which the pH dependent effects on soil and plant availability inter-relate is sulphate. Its adsorption onto soil decreases strongly with increasing pH and so "soil availability" increases with increasing pH but at the same time plant uptake of sulphate decreases strongly with increasing pH and so "plant availability" decreases. An example of a nutrient for which the effect of pH on plant availability is greater than on soil availability is phosphate where the decrease in plant uptake with increasing pH appears to dominate the increase in soil availability resulting from the decrease in phosphate adsorption with increasing pH. However the increase in soil availability is small relative to the large decrease in adsorption of molybdate with increasing pH which in turn dominates any increase in plant uptake with increasing pH. Zinc and copper adsorption increase with increasing pH but at the same time plant uptake of the ions also increases so that availability decreases only slightly with increasing pH. That of boron is quite different. The effects of pH on boron adsorption are small and even at low pH values boric acid is the predominant boron species. Its uptake by plant roots does not depend on charge as boric acid molecules dominate at the root surface and the rate of uptake of boron by plants is proportional to the concentration of the boric acid species. Our views are based on a review of variable charge soils and crops. It is our opinion that the importance of the influence of pH on nutrient reactions with soils has been overstated at the expense of the importance of the influences of pH on plant availability of nutrients.
